title: Indian Financial Market Structure: Money vs. Capital Market
focus: Distinctions between Money Market and Capital Market, their regulators, and key instruments.
rationale: Fundamental economic concepts frequently tested in UPSC prelims, specifically the differences in duration, regulators, and instruments between money and capital markets.
sectionExcerpt: The Indian Financial Market is divided into Money Market and Capital Market. The Money Market caters to short-term needs (less than 1 year), is regulated by RBI, and includes instruments like Treasury Bills, Call Money, Commercial Paper, and Certificate of Deposits. The Capital Market is for long-term wealth creation (greater than 1 year), is regulated by SEBI, and includes Primary (IPO) and Secondary (Stock Market) markets dealing in shares and bonds.
evidenceBullets: Money Market duration is less than 1 year and is regulated by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI).
Money Market instruments include Treasury Bills (T-Bills), Call Money, Commercial Paper (CP), and Certificate of Deposits (CDs).
Capital Market duration is greater than 1 year, involves high risk, and is regulated by SEBI.
Capital Market includes the Primary Market for new issues (IPO) and the Secondary Market for trading shares and bonds.

title: Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) and Recent Initiatives
focus: SEBI's statutory status, board composition, and recent frameworks like SWAGAT-FI, REITs, and InvITs.
rationale: SEBI's regulatory powers, board structure, and new frameworks like SWAGAT-FI and REIT/InvIT distinctions are highly probable areas for regulatory body questions.
sectionExcerpt: SEBI was established in 1988 and became a statutory body under the SEBI Act of 1992. It has civil court powers for enforcement. Its board includes nominees from the Union Government, Finance Ministry, and RBI. Recently, SEBI introduced the SWAGAT-FI framework for Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) and Foreign Venture Capital Investors (FVCIs). It also reclassified Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) as Equity and relaxed IPO norms. InvITs are similar to REITs but invest in infrastructure.
evidenceBullets: SEBI became a statutory body after the SEBI Act of 1992 and possesses civil court powers under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
The SEBI Board includes a chairman, two Finance Ministry members, one RBI member, and five Union Government nominees.
The SWAGAT-FI framework was introduced for FPIs and Foreign Venture Capital Investors (FVCIs).
REITs allow investment in income-producing real estate, while InvITs invest in infrastructure assets like roads and power lines.

title: Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs)
focus: Definition, regulation, and the three categories of Alternative Investment Funds in India.
rationale: AIF categories (I, II, III) and their specific investment targets (startups vs. debt vs. hedge funds) are standard UPSC economy targets.
sectionExcerpt: Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs) are privately pooled investment vehicles regulated by SEBI. RBI exempted the government-backed SWAMIH Fund from tightened AIF rules. AIFs are divided into three categories: Category I invests in start-ups, SMEs, and infrastructure; Category II does not use leverage (e.g., Private Equity, Debt, Real Estate Funds); Category III may use leverage and includes Hedge Funds and PIPE.
evidenceBullets: AIFs are privately pooled investment vehicles regulated by SEBI under the 2012 regulations.
Category I AIFs invest in start-ups, early-stage ventures, SMEs, and infrastructure funds.
Category II AIFs do not use leverage and include Private Equity Funds, Debt Funds, and Real Estate Funds.
Category III AIFs may use leverage and include Hedge Funds and Private investment in public equity (PIPE).

title: Equity Derivatives and Front Running
focus: Definitions and mechanisms of equity derivatives and the illegal practice of front running.
rationale: Basic financial market terminology and market manipulation practices are often tested as concept-based questions.
sectionExcerpt: A Derivative is a financial instrument whose value is derived from an underlying asset like equity, metals, or currencies. Futures and Options are common equity derivatives. Front Running is an illegal market manipulation practice where a security is purchased based on advanced non-public information regarding an expected large transaction, undermining market integrity.
evidenceBullets: Derivatives derive their value from underlying assets such as equity shares, commodities, or currencies.
Futures and Options are the most commonly-traded equity derivatives products.
Front Running is an illegal practice of trading based on advanced non-public information about a large pending transaction to manipulate prices.
